title: Deep Learning Recommendation Model for Personalization and Recommendation Systems (DLRM)
authors: Maxim Naumov, Dheevatsa Mudigere, Hao-Jun Michael Shi, Jianyu Huang, Narayanan Sundaraman, Jongsoo Park, Xiaodong Wang, Udit Gupta, Carole-Jean Wu, Misha Smelyanskiy et al. (Facebook AI)
venue: arXiv:1906.00091 [cs.IR] (preprint; open-sourced at github.com/facebookresearch/dlrm). Later widely adopted as the MLPerf recommendation benchmark.
tokenization_method: Per-categorical-field learned embedding table lookup combined with an MLP-projected dense-feature vector, then explicit second-order feature interaction by pairwise dot product. Concretely: (1) each of the 26 categorical (sparse) fields i has its own embedding table W_i in R^{m_i x d}; a lookup is e_i^T W_i = w_i (a one-hot, or a multi-hot/weighted-pooled sum AT W per nn.EmbeddingBag/SparseLengthsSum for multi-valued fields). (2) The 13 continuous (dense) features go through a 'bottom MLP' that projects them to a single vector of the SAME dimension d as the embeddings, so the dense block becomes one extra token of width d. (3) All field vectors (26 embeddings + 1 dense-MLP vector) are interacted by taking the dot product of every pair, mimicking a factorization machine; the resulting scalars (strictly upper-triangular set) are concatenated with the original dense-MLP vector and fed to a 'top MLP' + sigmoid to give the click probability. So tokenization = one fixed-width-d vector per field, and feature interaction = explicit pairwise dot products rather than letting an MLP discover them.
feature_types_handled: numerical (13 dense continuous features, log(1+x) pre-transform) and categorical (26 sparse fields, including high-cardinality). Multi-hot / multi-valued categorical fields are supported natively via pooled embedding-bag sums. No dedicated temporal tokenizer (time is implicit in the chronological train/val/test day split of Criteo, not modeled as a sequence). Missing/unlabeled categoricals are mapped to index 0 / NULL.
token_granularity: per-feature (per-field): exactly one d-dimensional vector per categorical field and one d-dimensional vector for the whole dense block (produced by the bottom MLP). NOT per-value and NOT field-value-pair. Embedding dimension d is shared across all fields (d=16 in the Criteo Kaggle accuracy experiment; d=64 in the systems-profiling config) so that all field tokens are dot-product-compatible.
numerical_strategy: log(1+x) transform then a shared bottom MLP that maps the concatenated 13 dense features to one d-dim vector. This is NOT per-numerical-feature embedding (contrast On-Embeddings/PLR/AutoDis): all dense features are squashed into a single token, so the model cannot apply explicit pairwise interaction between two individual numerical features (they are pre-mixed by the bottom MLP) — only between the dense block as-a-whole and each categorical field.
categorical_strategy: learned dense embedding table per field, trained end-to-end on the CTR target (logistic/cross-entropy). For multi-valued fields, embedding-bag pooling (sum) of the looked-up rows. Equivalent to the entity-embeddings primitive (Guo & Berkhahn 2016) applied at industrial scale.
high_cardinality_handling: Each high-cardinality field gets its own large embedding table (the paper profiles tables of 1M rows each; production tables are stated to exceed multiple GBs each, dominating total parameters at ~540M for the Criteo-sized model). The embedding tables ARE the scaling pain point — handled at the SYSTEMS level (model-parallel sharding of tables across devices + personalized all-to-all 'butterfly shuffle' communication) rather than at the representation level. No hashing, no frequency bucketing, no shared/compositional codebook is used in this paper — every category index gets a dedicated row. The Criteo categorical indices are pre-hashed by the dataset itself, not by DLRM.
temporal_handling: none as a tokenizer. Criteo is intrinsically temporal (7-day Kaggle / 24-day Terabyte click logs) and the paper uses a chronological split (train on days 1..n-1, val/test on the last day), but there is no recurrence, patching, or temporal positional signal — each row is treated i.i.d. Time-of-day / recency would have to be injected as ordinary dense or categorical features.
scales_to_many_features: Scales to ~39 fields here (13 dense + 26 categorical) but the explicit pairwise dot-product interaction is O(F^2) in the number of FIELDS, F. At 39 fields this is cheap; at 70+ heterogeneous features (and especially if dense features were tokenized individually rather than pre-pooled) the number of interaction tokens grows quadratically. The genuine scaling story in the paper is about PARAMETER/MEMORY scale (embedding tables in the GBs), solved by combined model-parallel (embeddings) + data-parallel (MLPs) training with all-to-all communication — NOT about scaling the number of interacting tokens. So 'scales' here means 'scales to web-scale data and huge embedding tables', not 'scales to hundreds of interacting feature tokens'.
downstream_model: bottom MLP (dense features -> d-dim token) + explicit pairwise dot-product interaction layer + top MLP + sigmoid. Criteo Kaggle config: bottom MLP 512-256-64, top MLP 512-256, d=16, ~540M params. Systems-profiling config: 8 categorical x 1M-row tables, d=64, 512 dense features, bottom MLP 2 layers, top MLP 4 layers. PyTorch (nn.EmbeddingBag, addmm, bmm) and Caffe2 (SparseLengthSum, FC, BatchMatMul) implementations.
pretraining_objective: none. Fully supervised end-to-end on the CTR binary label (cross-entropy). Embeddings are shaped purely by the supervised target; no self-supervision, no transfer, no foundation-model pretraining.
key_results: On Criteo Ad Kaggle (~45M samples, 7 days), DLRM obtains slightly higher training AND validation accuracy than a Deep & Cross Network (DCN) sized to ~540M params, over one epoch, with both SGD and Adagrad, NO regularization, and explicitly 'without extensive tuning'. Accuracy is reported only as a curve (~0.78-0.79 accuracy region); no AUC/logloss table is given. Systems result: the single-socket profiling config runs ~256 s (CPU) / ~62 s (GPU) in Caffe2, with embedding lookups + FC layers dominating time (FC negligible on GPU, significant on CPU). The lasting contribution is the open-source model + benchmark, not a SOTA accuracy claim.
baselines_compared: Deep & Cross Network (DCN, Wang et al. 2017) on Criteo Kaggle (the only quantitative comparison). Qualitatively positioned against Wide&Deep, DeepFM, xDeepFM, Neural Collaborative Filtering (NCF), and classical matrix-factorization / factorization-machine baselines. No GBDT baseline, no FT-Transformer-style baseline (predates them in this venue).
transferable_idea: Make every heterogeneous field emit ONE fixed-width-d token (a learned embedding for each categorical field; a single MLP-projected vector for the whole dense block) so that all fields live in a common d-dim space, then model interaction EXPLICITLY by pairwise dot products instead of relying on a deep MLP to discover them. The 'project dense features into the same token space as categorical embeddings, then dot-product-interact' recipe, plus 'one embedding table per high-cardinality field, sharded model-parallel', is the directly reusable kernel for a 70+-field tokenizer — provided F^2 interaction cost stays bounded.
limitations: (1) Only second-order interactions are modeled (the paper argues higher-order may not be worth the cost — an empirical bet, not proven for all data). (2) All 13 dense features are collapsed into ONE token by the bottom MLP, so individual numerical features cannot interact pairwise with each other and there is no per-numerical-feature embedding (no piecewise-linear/periodic encoding). (3) Single quantitative comparison (vs DCN, one epoch, untuned) is a weak accuracy claim — the paper's own framing is 'benchmark / system co-design', not 'we win accuracy'. (4) No temporal modeling, no missing-value strategy beyond index-0, no cold-start/OOV handling for unseen categories. (5) Embedding tables dominate memory (GBs), forcing custom model-parallel infrastructure not natively supported by PyTorch/Caffe2.
pitfalls: (1) 'Scales to large models' conflates two things: web-scale DATA + huge embedding TABLES (which it genuinely addresses via sharding) vs many interacting FEATURE TOKENS (which it does NOT — pairwise dot product is O(F^2) in fields). Do not cite DLRM as evidence that the architecture scales to 70+ INTERACTING tokens. (2) The accuracy edge over DCN is from a single untuned one-epoch run with no regularization and no AUC/logloss numbers — treat the 'DLRM > DCN' claim as suggestive, not established; later literature does not consistently rank DLRM above DCN-style models. (3) Collapsing all dense features into one bottom-MLP token is a design choice optimized for CTR with few dense fields (13); copying it for a numerics-heavy 70+-feature table throws away per-numerical-feature resolution that the numerical-embeddings route shows matters. (4) Criteo categoricals arrive pre-hashed/anonymized, so DLRM never has to solve raw high-cardinality string encoding itself — its 'high-cardinality handling' is really 'big embedding table for an already-integerized id space'.
relevance_to_topic: The canonical industrial reference for tokenizing many heterogeneous (numerical + high-cardinality categorical) fields at production scale: it operationalizes 'one learned token per field + explicit dot-product interaction + model-parallel embedding sharding', which is the closest production-grade analogue to the user's 70+-mixed-feature setting, while exposing exactly where field-level (not value-level) numerical tokenization and O(F^2) interaction break down.
code_available: yes — https://github.com/facebookresearch/dlrm (official Facebook Research PyTorch/Caffe2 reference; maintained, reproducible Criteo Kaggle/Terabyte pipeline; the MLPerf recommendation reference implementation).
